title: Easy Kitchen Island
summary: A slatted-shelf kitchen island built from two square-checked 2x4 frames — prefinished slats, a 2x6 tabletop, and every joint sized so a Kreg Jig is a nice-to-have, not a requirement.
description:
This island is deliberately written to work whether or not you own a pocket-hole jig — every joint has both a pocket-screw version and a self-tapping-screw version, and neither one is the "real" way to build it. That flexibility is what makes this a genuinely good first big furniture project.

The two end frames are the foundation everything else references, and the plan's own tip is worth following exactly: check square by measuring both diagonals and adjusting until they match, and add the shelf slats from a later step now if it helps hold the frame square while you work. A frame that racks even slightly before the legs go on stays racked for the rest of the build.

Leg attachment gets one small but real detail — drive the screws at a slight angle rather than straight in, which meaningfully improves the joint's structural strength over a dead-perpendicular screw. The shelf slats are prefinished BEFORE cutting, specifically so the only touch-up needed after assembly is the cut ends — finishing a shelf full of installed slats one at a time is a much worse afternoon.

The tabletop supports do double duty: lateral bracing for the whole island, and the attachment point the tabletop screws into from below. Build the top separately with a pocket-hole jig if you have one — a solid pre-joined top attaches cleanly in one step, while building it in place is fussier.

tools.note: Every joint has a no-jig alternative built into this plan.
Checks the frames for square by comparing diagonals.

materials.name: Self-tapping screws or pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"

cutList.note: Prefinish before cutting so only the cut ends need touch-up later.

steps.title: Build the two frames
steps.body:
Build two frames, either with pocket holes or with 2-1/2" self-tapping star bit screws. Check each frame for square by measuring opposite diagonals and adjusting until they match.

Adding the shelf slats from a later step now can help keep the frames square while you work — a frame that racks before the legs go on stays racked.
materials: Self-tapping screws or pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"

steps.title: Add the legs
steps.body: Attach the legs from the inside with 2-1/2" screws — pocket-hole users can simply drive pocket screws here instead. Drive the screws at a slight angle rather than straight in; it makes for a noticeably better structural joint.
materials: Self-tapping screws or pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"

steps.title: Add the shelf supports
steps.body: Measure and mark the placement of the 2x2 shelf supports and attach with 2-1/2" screws.
materials: Self-tapping screws or pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"

steps.title: Slat the shelf
steps.body: Prefinish the slat boards before cutting, so only the cut ends need touch-up after assembly. Lay the slats out with even gaps and nail and glue them to the frame boards. Other stock — 1x3, 1x2, even 1x6 or 1x8 — substitutes if you'd rather a different look.

steps.title: Add the tabletop supports
steps.body: Attach the tabletop support boards, which give the island lateral bracing and an attachment point for the tabletop. Attach from inside with pocket holes facing upward if you have a jig, otherwise screw from the outside.
materials: Self-tapping screws or pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"

steps.title: Attach the tabletop
steps.body: For the cleanest result, join the tabletop boards into one panel with a pocket-hole jig first, then attach the whole top from underneath with 2-1/2" screws. Without a jig, attach the boards individually the same way, keeping them tight and even.
materials: Self-tapping screws or pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"

images.alt: Simple kitchen island with a slatted lower shelf and 2x6 tabletop
